title: Quantum walk-based optimisation for capacitated vehicle routing with homogeneous and heterogeneous fleets
authors: Edric Matwiejew, Aidan Smith, Callum Neill, Paulo Santos, Ugo Varetto, Jingbo Wang
abstract: The capacitated vehicle routing problem (CVRP) is an appealing candidate for quantum optimisation due to its combinatorial complexity and practical importance. However, the problem's constrained search space poses a challenge for such quantum algorithms. We introduce a quantum walk-based optimisation algorithm (QWOA) for the CVRP with homogeneous or heterogeneous vehicle fleets, addressing this challenge through a continuous-time quantum walk over a product space that coincides with combinatorial structures intrinsic to the CVRP solution space. Relative to the prior QWOA-based formulation, this approach reduces the per-layer gate complexity from $\mathcal{O}(n^{3}\log n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n^{2}\log n)$ and supports a circuit parameterisation schedule generated by a fixed number of classical parameters. Exact state-vector simulation on instances with up to $n=8$ customers and $K=3$ vehicles demonstrates improved convergence to low-cost solutions using markedly fewer objective function evaluations, with the advantage broadening as problem size increases. These results identify structured product-space walks as a promising tool for optimisation over constrained combinatorial spaces.
summary: This paper develops and compares two quantum walk algorithms (PS-QWOA vs. I-QWOA) for solving the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP). The PS-QWOA is shown to be theoretically superior because it efficiently encodes the problem's combinatorial constraints into a product space, leading to better convergence rates and lower computational overhead compared to prior methods.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of optimizing the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), an NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem, using quantum computation.
main_result: The Product-Space Quantum Walk-Based Optimization Algorithm (PS-QWOA) consistently outperforms the Indexed QWOA (I-QWOA) across various performance metrics, showing superior probability concentration on low-cost feasible solutions.
method: The authors adapt quantum walk theory by constructing a continuous-time quantum walk over a product space encoding CVRP solutions. They use variational optimization techniques to minimize an objective function related to the problem cost.
model_or_system: The model is the PS-QWOA, which uses a Hamiltonian derived from graph structures (transposition and Hamming graphs) acting on a product Hilbert space ($\mathcal{H}_\pi \otimes \mathcal{H}_a$). The dynamics are governed by interleaved unitary operations.
key_observables: Feasibility Probability ($P_{	ext{feas}}$), Optimal-Feasible Amplification ($\mathcal{A}_{	ext{opt}}$), and the classical optimization cost (number of objective function evaluations).
important_parameters: Number of customers ($n$), number of vehicles ($K$), circuit depth ($p$), and penalty parameters ($\lambda$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on exact state-vector simulation for small instances ($n \le 8$) and assumes the applicability of structured product-space walks to constrained combinatorial spaces.
figures_summary: Figures compare performance metrics like $\Delta P_{	ext{feas}}$ and $\Delta \log_{10} \mathcal{A}_{	ext{opt}}$ between PS-QWOA and I-QWOA, showing the advantage of PS-QWOA as problem size increases. Other figures detail graph structures and cost distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CVRP formulation, details the PS-QWOA ansatz by defining its product space structure ($	ext{G}_\pi + 	ext{G}_a$), compares it against I-QWOA using rigorous metrics ($P_{	ext{feas}}, \mathcal{A}_{	ext{opt}}$), and concludes that PS-QWOA is superior due to better scaling and lower classical overhead.
why_interesting: The work provides a concrete, structured quantum algorithm for a classically hard combinatorial problem by mapping constraints onto the structure of the quantum walk's product space, which has implications for designing resource-efficient quantum circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of a CVRP instance. The central depot dispatches vehicles along distinct routes (coloured paths) to serve customers (numbered nodes). Each node is annotated with its demand, and each edge with its travel cost. Every route must begin and end at the depot, and the total demand on each route must not exceed the vehicle capacity.

Figure 2. PS-QWOA walk-generator graphs for n = 3 and K = 2. Left: with π = (1, 2, 3) fixed, Ga produces the assignment graph H(3, 2) on the eight possible assignments, denoted as a1a2a3. Centre: with a = (1, 1, 2) fixed, Gπ produces the transposition graph on S3. Colours distinguishing the three transpositions (12), (13), and (23). Right: the composite generator Ga + Gπ is a walk over the graph H(3, 2)□S3, where □denotes the graph Cartesian product.

Figure 3. The PS-QWOA circuit for the CVRP, showing the separability of the state preparation (Eq. (16)) and mixing unitary (Eq. (20)) over the assignment a and permutation π registers. Each of the p ansatz layers applies the phase- separation unitary (Eq. (7)) to the a and π registers, which evaluates the penalised problem cost function (Eq. (3)) on an O(b)-qubit ancilla register to precision O(2−b), followed by application of the mixing unitary.

Figure 4. Difference between the PS-QWOA and I-QWOA instance medians across the full simulated dataset. Rows are indexed by problem size (n, K) and are ordered by increasing effective Hilbert-space dimension |Heff|, with the smallest |Heff| at the top. Columns are indexed by the circuit depth p = 1, . . . , 8. Top row: Difference in feasibility probability ∆Pfeas for homogeneous CVRP (left) and heterogeneous CVRP (right) instances. Bottom row: Difference in log10 of the optimal-feasible amplification ∆log10 Aopt. Each cell is the difference between the PS-QWOA and I-QWOA medians over the five problem instances. Positive values (red) indicate a PS-QWOA advantage.

Figure 5. Penalised-cost amplification profile A(fλ) = P(fλ)/Punif(fλ) of optimised PS-QWOA and I-QWOA states for a single problem instance at depth p = 8. Top: homoge- neous CVRP (n, K) = (7, 3). Bottom: heterogeneous CVRP (n, K) = (7, 3).
